title: Stop VOYEURS Act of 2025
plain_summary: This bill makes it easier for the federal government to prosecute people who secretly record videos of others in private situations. Currently, federal law only applies when this happens on government property, but the new law would cover many more situations like when people travel between states or use the internet. The bill also increases the maximum prison sentence from one year to five years. The law is meant to better protect people from being secretly recorded without permission.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

sections.title: Section 1  Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Stop Victimizers and Offenders from Yielding Explicit Unconsented Recordings Surreptitiously Act of two thousand twenty five. It can also be called the Stop VOYEURS Act of two thousand twenty five.

sections.title: Section 2  Expanded Prohibition Against Video Voyeurism
sections.text: This section changes an existing law about video voyeurism (secretly recording people without permission). The law currently only applies in certain federal areas like military bases and national parks. This bill expands the law to cover many more situations. The punishment increases from up to one year in prison to up to five years in prison. The new law applies when any of these things happen. First, when either the person doing the recording or the victim traveled between states or to other countries, or used something that crosses state lines like the internet or mail, to help with the illegal recording. Second, when the person doing the recording used the internet, mail, phones, or other systems that cross state lines to help with the illegal recording. Third, when any payment was made using banks, credit cards, or other financial systems that work across state lines to help with the illegal recording. Fourth, when the person doing the recording sent any messages across state lines using computers, mail, phones, or other communication systems related to the illegal recording. Fifth, when any equipment used for the recording had traveled across state lines, like cameras or phones made in other states. Sixth, when the illegal recording happened in federal areas like military bases, national parks, or US territories. Seventh, when the illegal recording affected business or activities that cross state lines in any other way.
